title: Phase-correlation-free quantum key distribution source operating at gigahertz rates
authors: Shashank Kumar, Alessandro Marcomini, Loïc Millet, Towsif Taher, Aurélien Cavalié, Raphael Houlmann, David Cabrerizo, Gianluca Boso, Marcos Curty, Rob Thew, Boris Korzh
abstract: Phase randomization is essential for the security of practical decoy-state quantum key distribution (QKD) systems. Commonly, implementations rely on laser sources which are either actively phase-randomized, or gain-switched. However, at high repetition rates these show correlations, which can ultimately compromise security and performance. We present a 1.25 GHz phase-randomized QKD source based on a super-luminescent light emitting diode (SLED) operating in the C-band as a compact and cost-effective alternative. The source generates $\sim100$ ps optical pulses with $400$ ps pulse-to-pulse separation, compatible with high-speed time-bin encoding. Interferometric measurements demonstrate $>99\%$ visibility between adjacent time bins, confirming strong first-order coherence within the same quantum signals, while the spontaneous-emission-driven nature of the SLED ensures intrinsic global phase randomization between adjacent signals. This work establishes a scalable SLED-based platform for high-speed prepare-and-measure QKD systems.
summary: This paper introduces a Super-Luminescent Light Emitting Diode (SLED) as a viable, low-cost source for high-speed Quantum Key Distribution (QKD). It experimentally proves that the SLED inherently randomizes the phase between successive pulses, solving a major security hurdle in QKD. Furthermore, it demonstrates the ability to engineer the necessary intra-signal coherence for time-bin encoding, establishing a scalable platform for next-generation quantum communication.
main_problem: The primary challenge is developing a secure, high-speed Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) source that avoids phase correlations inherent in conventional laser sources at gigahertz repetition rates.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a Super-Luminescent Light Emitting Diode (SLED) can serve as a compact, cost-effective source that achieves intrinsic global phase randomization between adjacent pulses while maintaining high intra-signal coherence necessary for time-bin encoding.
method: The work involves characterizing the SLED source using interferometric measurements (1DLI for phase correlation and 2DLI for intra-signal coherence) and simulating the performance using decoy-state BB84 protocols.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a C-band SLED operating at high rates (e.g., 1.25 GHz). The source's spontaneous-emission-driven nature provides intrinsic phase randomization, which is combined with active modulation (pulse carving and basis encoding) to generate time-bin states.
key_observables: Visibility ($V_{1DLI}$ and $V_{2DLI}$), extinction ratio ($\approx 25$ dB), and the second-order correlation function ($g_2(0)$).
important_parameters: Repetition rates up to 1.25 GHz, pulse widths ($\sim 100$ ps), and the effective number of temporal modes ($M$).
main_assumptions: The security analysis relies on the SLED's ability to mimic the performance of an ideal phase-randomized source, and the performance is compared against theoretical Poissonian limits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup for phase correlation measurements (1DLI) and time-bin signal generation (2DLI), comparing visibility results between the SLED and a reference laser source.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need for phase randomization, then presenting the SLED as a solution, followed by detailed measurements confirming both pulse-to-pulse randomization and intra-signal coherence, and concluding with performance comparisons for QKD.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it proposes a practical, solid-state alternative to complex laser systems for quantum communication, directly addressing decoherence and correlation issues in high-speed quantum protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. Visibility comparison for laser and SLED sources. (a) Nearest-neighbour interference (one-DLI configuration), determin- ing the global phase relation of pulses in different rounds of the protocol. (b) X-basis measurement (two-DLI configuration), corre- sponding to a measurement of the coherence among time-bins within the same signal. Counts N are normalized as N/Nmax −(Nmax + Nmin)/2, being Nmax (Nmin) the maximum (minimum) counts of each curve.

Figure 1. Experimental setup for phase-correlation measurements. (a) Pulse-carved signals at 2.5 GHz are sent to a matched delay-line interferometer (DLI) to test the phase coherence between neighbor- ing pulses by monitoring for constructive and destructive interference at the outputs. The relative phase is controlled by a voltage bias. (b) Pulses carved at 833 MHz are sent through a first DLI to generate time-bin signals (i.e., early and late time bins with 400 ps separa- tion). A second matched DLI recombines the two bins to measure their relative phase coherence. The outputs are detected using super- conducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) connected to a time-to-digital...

Figure 3. Time-domain measurement of the prepared states. (a) Profile of the state-encoding IM applied to a continuous-wave sig- nal from the SLED. For Z-basis measurements, one time-bin is fully suppressed (with an extinction ratio exceeding 25 dB), while for the X-basis a balanced superposition of the time-bins is encoded. (b) Fi- nal states obtained by combining the encoder of panel (a) with pulse- carving. The resulting pulses have a full-width half-maximum of about 120 ps.

Figure 4. (a) Photon counts versus time with the HBT experiment for the SLED source filtered through the DWDM channel. The primary peak corresponds to zero-time coincidences, and is purposely delayed to capture the full peak shape. The Nside = 6 secondary peaks occur periodically, in accordance with the source pulsing rate. The g2(0) value is obtained through Eq. 6 considering an integration window of 100 ps. (b) Scaling of g2(0) estimates upon changing the channel bandwidth through the OSA tunable filter, with the corresponding fit given by Eq. 4 with M = γ (approximate fit) and M = M(γ) (Eq. 5, full fit), where γ ∝∆λch. For each measurement, the corresponding effective mode number M is...

Figure 5. Asymptotic secret key rate obtainable from a phase- randomized laser source (Poissonian statistics, dotted black line), compared with that of multimodal thermal light characteristic of the SLED for different values of the effective mode number M (solid blue lines). The red dashed line corresponds to the conservative es- timate of M from the experimental data in Fig. 4(b). As M ≫1, the multimode-thermal distribution approaches the Poissonian, and re- sults correctly converge for M = 100.
